format: ALEX/JORDAN dialogue, plain text, deep-dive (~30 minute target)
structure: Take-two follow-up to 2026-05-02_deepdive_friedson — comparison axis (Friedson vs Glass vs Jawando across 6 topics) + open-questions resolution pass
topics_compared: Property tax (Friedson most consistent no, Glass close no, Jawando more open to rate)
Schools-fiscal (Friedson/Glass willing to cap at existing rate, Jawando willing to raise rate)
Housing (Friedson supply-side lead sponsor, Glass moderate yes, Jawando no on More Housing N.O.W.)
Climate (Friedson + Glass coalition on Bill 2-26 Green Bank, Jawando supportive but not lead)
Immigration (all 3 are co-sponsors of Trust Act + County Values Act — split is emphasis only)
Public safety / police in schools (Friedson yes, Jawando no, Glass undecided publicly)
open_questions_resolved_partial: Bill 2-25 status: still not vote-count surfaced — kept as open
Homeowner-tax-credit dollar impact: ~$500-$1500/yr per eligible household, ~$3.2M total county fiscal impact in FY25
FY27 budget gap: still no candidate has put a dollar number on it
2018-vs-2026 Friedson ideological position: not substantially shifted — same fiscal-discipline / TOD / good-government lane
Equity mitigation in police-in-schools letter: implicit-bias training, demographic arrest reporting, community advisory board
new_material_today: Cash-on-hand: Friedson ~$2.1M cycle-to-date, Glass ~$1.1M (incl public match), Jawando ~$0.9M (incl public match)
Labor split: SEIU 500 + UFCW 27 to Jawando, MCEA to Glass, IAFF 1664 to Friedson, FOP 35 pending
Editorial endorsements timeline: GGW endorsed Friedson; Bethesda Magazine and Washington Post still ahead
fabrication_check: Cash-on-hand and labor breakdown pulled from public State Board of Elections filings and union-endorsement press releases as of 2026-05-02. If any specific dollar figure or endorsement attribution is contested, the script frames numbers as 'approximately' to allow for the most recent filing not yet being on the dossier.
